scenario: You sit down and your HUD shows this opponent over a decent sample.
question: What player type is this?
explanation: Low VPIP/PFR (15/12) with a small gap, plus a high fold-to-cbet — classic Nit. They play only premium hands and fold when they miss. Respect their aggression (they only bet the near-nuts) but steal relentlessly when they show weakness.

scenario: Your HUD on this player over 200 hands.
hud: VPIP 50 · PFR 8 · Fold to CBet 15% · Fold to 3Bet 12%
question: What player type is this?
explanation: High VPIP (50) with low PFR (8) = loose-passive. The giveaway for a calling STATION specifically is the very low fold-to-cbet (15%) and fold-to-3bet (12%) — they hate to fold. Never bluff this player. Value bet relentlessly and size up: they'll call with bottom pair or ace-high.

question: What player type is this?
explanation: VPIP 65 / PFR 53 is extreme — far looser and more aggressive than even Villain (LAG) (30/28). This is a Maniac: uncontrolled aggression, betting and raising relentlessly without a sound plan. Don't try to out-bluff them. Trap with strong hands and let them barrel into you — they'll stack off light.

scenario: You hold A♣J♦ on J♥ 7♠ 3♣ (top pair top kicker). You bet the flop, Villain (nit) CALLS. Turn 2♦, you bet again, Villain (nit) RAISES big.
question: Villain (nit) just raised your turn bet. What do you do with top pair top kicker?
options: Fold — Villain (nit)'s raise is the near-nuts
explanation: This is the textbook hero-fold spot. Villain (nit) raising the turn means a set, two pair, or better — they do not bluff and don't raise worse for value. Hardin: 'If there is ever a time to make a big hero fold with top-pair-top-kicker, it's against Villain (nit).' TPTK is a strong hand, but against THIS player it's beat. Fold.

scenario: You hold A♣J♦ on J♥ 7♠ 3♣ (top pair top kicker). The board is dry.
question: It's checked to you on the flop against Villain (calling station). Best line with TPTK?
options: Check — pot control vs a tough player
Bet large for value — they call with worse
Bet small to keep them in
explanation: Against Villain (calling station), value bet LARGE with your strong hands. They call with bottom pair, ace-high, gutshots — hands far worse than your TPTK. The mistake players make is betting small or checking 'to control the pot.' No — extract maximum. They won't fold, so charge them. Never bluff them, always value-bet thin and big.

scenario: You hold A♣J♦ on J♥ 7♠ 3♣ (top pair top kicker). You bet flop, Villain (lag) calls. Turn 2♦, you check, Villain (lag) BETS big.
question: Villain (LAG) bets after you check the turn. What do you do with TPTK?
options: Fold — they have a strong range
Call — their betting range is full of bluffs
explanation: Against Villain (LAG), do NOT respect aggression the way you would Villain (nit). Their betting range includes mediocre made hands, weak draws, and pure bluffs. TPTK is a clear call — you beat the large bluffing portion of their range. Hardin: make hero calls vs LAGs you'd never make vs Villain (nit). Same hand, opposite decision from Villain (nit) spot above.

scenario: You raised preflop and missed completely: A♥ K♥ on 8♣ 6♠ 2♦. You c-bet, Villain (station) calls. Turn is a blank. You have ace-high, no draw.
question: Should you fire a second barrel as a bluff?
options: Yes — they might fold this time
Only if the river is scary
explanation: Never bluff Villain (calling station). They hate to fold and will call you down with bottom pair or ace-high. A second barrel here just spews chips. Give up, check it down, and hope your ace-high is good. Save your bets for when you have a real hand — then value bet large.

scenario: You're the preflop aggressor. Flop comes and you both have wide ranges. Villain (nit) checks to you on a dry board.
question: You have a marginal hand. Best play?
options: C-bet — they fold far too often
explanation: Exploit Villain (nit)'s biggest leak: they fold to a high percentage of c-bets. Bet relentlessly on dry boards — your actual hand barely matters because they fold so often when they miss. When they DON'T fold (call or raise), believe them and shut down. The fold equity alone makes c-betting hugely profitable here.

scenario: Folded to you on the BTN. The blinds are both nits who fold their blinds very often.
question: How should you adjust your BTN opening range?
options: Tighten — they only play strong hands
Widen significantly — steal their blinds relentlessly
explanation: Steal relentlessly. Nits fold their blinds at a high rate, so open a much wider range than standard from the BTN — the dead money is yours for the taking. This is one of the most profitable adjustments in poker: attacking tight players' blinds. If they wake up with a 3-bet, give them credit and fold the junk.

scenario: Villain (calling station) limps in front of you. You're on the BTN with a decent-but-not-premium hand like K♠T♠.
question: Best approach?
options: Isolation-raise larger than normal for value
explanation: Iso-raise, and size UP. You want to play heads-up in position against their weak, wide calling range — and since they over-call, build the pot now with a hand that dominates much of their range. Bigger sizing exploits their inability to fold. Don't limp behind and let others in; isolate the fish.

scenario: You have a flush draw: A♥5♥ on K♥8♥2♣. Villain (nit) leads big into you on the flop.
question: How do you read Villain (nit)'s big lead, and what's your play?
options: They have a strong made hand — but your draw + overcard has equity, call with proper odds
explanation: Villain (nit) leading big = strong made hand (set, two pair, top pair strong kicker), almost never a bluff. But you have the nut flush draw plus an ace overcard — real equity. The read tells you you're behind NOW, but your draw justifies continuing if the pot odds are right. Don't raise (they won't fold), just call and realize your equity. Reading the type tells you it's a made hand; the math tells you to draw.
